raw_text:
As COVID-19 vaccines are distributed, individuals who are trying to get pregnant or pregnant may have questions about the vaccines. At the
University of Massachusetts Medical School – Baystate
, a group of experts (the
Shared Decision-Making: COVID Vaccination in Pregnancy working group
), created an aid to help individuals who are pregnant, lactating, or planning on becoming pregnant decide whether or not to receive an mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. These
decision aids
, currently available in 10 languages, will be updated on this post as more information. Additional information on the safety of COVID-19 vaccines in pregnancy can be found
here
.

ai_summary.summary: A working group of physicians at the University of Massachusetts Medical School – Baystate created multilingual decision-making guides to help pregnant, breastfeeding, or pregnancy-planning individuals evaluate whether to receive an mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. The guides are available in 10 languages including Spanish, Chinese, Somali, and Vietnamese. The materials were developed by emergency medicine and obstetrics specialists and are based on published research and guidance from the CDC, FDA, and professional medical organizations.

ai_summary.summary: The Pennsylvania Senate Democratic Policy Committee, chaired by Senator Katie Muth, hosted a series of public hearings throughout 2024 on various policy topics including property reassessment, election reform, housing and homelessness, PFAS contamination, healthcare access, pharmacy closures, deaths in custody, eviction records, home repairs, and water rates. These hearings were held across multiple Pennsylvania cities and often conducted jointly with other legislators. The hearings are informational events where lawmakers hear testimony but do not directly pass legislation.

Pa. just eliminated general assistance. The need for it hasn’t gone away
August 4, 2019
By Katie Muth, Maria Collett and Lindsey Williams
This week marks the end of General Assistance (GA) as we know it. The life-saving program that has helped thousands of Pennsylvanians get back on their feet and bridge the gap to self-sustainability ended on Aug. 1.
After a difficult battle through the legislative process and an underhanded maneuver to tie the elimination of GA to millions of dollars of hospitals, we accept that the program will cease to exist. However, that does not mean that the need has vanished.
read more
Lawmakers talking death penalty at State Capitol
May 3, 2019
Executions have become a rarity in Pennsylvania with Governor Tom Wolf instituting a moratorium on the death penalty. Now, some lawmakers want to take it a step further and abolish the death penalty in the state.
A Pennsylvania inmate has not been executed this century with the last one happening in 1999, but there are still plenty of inmates sitting on death row and some lawmakers think it’s doing more harm than good.

Pa. AG Shapiro holds roundtable on Title X at Planned Parenthood office
May 3, 2019
LOWER POTTSGROVE — Attorney General Josh Shapiro was in Lower Pottsgrove Friday to discuss reproductive rights and Title X funding.
“Every day we are fighting in court to protect women’s rights and particularly to protect healthcare and reproductive rights. Fights that we’ve won, 20, 30, 40 years ago, we’re fighting again,” Democrat Shapiro said to the group.

PA Legislators Propose Abolishing The Death Penalty
May 1, 2019
Democratic lawmakers in Pennsylvania have announced legislation that would abolish the death penalty in the state.
In presenting the Death Penalty Repeal Act, legislators argued that too many innocent inmates wound up death row due to an imperfect justice system.

Sen. Muth: End Pa. death penalty
May 1, 2019
A state senator whose district includes portions of Chester, Montgomery and Berks counties joined two Philadelphia legislators Tuesday in calling for the end of the death penalty in Pennsylvania.
State Sen Katie Muth, D-44th, of Royersford, announced the introduction of a bill that would repeal the use of capital punishment in the state, alongside state Sen. Sharif Street, D-3rd, and state Rep. Christopher Rabb, D-200th, both of Philadelphia.

ai_summary.summary: State Senator Katie Muth has engaged in a range of environmental advocacy actions over several years, including introducing water resource legislation, hosting public hearings on fracking and railroad safety, issuing statements on pipeline spills and cleanup failures, promoting renewable energy initiatives, and highlighting hazardous waste dangers to workers. She has also joined advocacy groups and fellow legislators in press conferences, webinars, and public hearings on topics including the Chesapeake Bay watershed, Marsh Creek Lake contamination, and oil and gas industry regulations. Some actions resulted in tangible outcomes such as nearly $1.9 million in DCNR grants for her district and $250,000 for a passenger rail feasibility study.

The grants were administered through various existing state programs and cover areas including fire and EMS services, infrastructure, housing, public safety, parks, and education. Muth's role in these announcements was to publicize funding decisions made by state agencies, not to introduce or pass legislation.

ai_summary.summary: The Pennsylvania Senate Democratic Policy Committee, chaired by Senator Katie Muth, held a series of public hearings throughout 2024 on various policy issues including property taxes, election reform, housing, homelessness, PFAS contamination, healthcare access, pharmacy closures, deaths in custody, eviction records, home repairs, and water rates. These hearings gathered testimony from witnesses but did not themselves create or pass any legislation. The committee held these events across multiple Pennsylvania cities including Harrisburg, Pittsburgh, Scranton, Lancaster, and others.

ai_summary.summary: State Senator Muth's office has published its 2026 schedule for mobile office hours at nine locations across the 44th District, including libraries, senior centers, and retirement communities. Staff will be available at these sites on a regular basis to help constituents with state services such as driver's license applications, property tax rebates, and vehicle registrations. A Veterans Service Officer will also hold monthly appointments at the Royersford district office.

ai_summary.summary: A Pennsylvania state senator's office published a webpage listing external scholarship, financial aid, and loan resources for constituents. The office compiled links to third-party organizations offering funding for college students, including programs for minorities, veterans, Polish-Americans, and others. The senator's office does not administer any of these programs and directs all inquiries to the listed organizations directly.
